story:
A woman wakes to voices that treat her like a case study. Eyes closed, she asks, “Am I awake yet?” She speaks from a landscape between sleep and pain: a cold window, a figure on the sill, glass tapping; birds outside in the snow, waiting to come in; shadows littering the room; a match that will not catch. The observers—scientists with clipboards and anxious whispers—tell each other to “note everything.” They call her Lydia. She cries when someone calls her “Dolly,” insists she hasn’t been anyone’s toy for a long time, and pleads for the figure to move. The birds are lonely. The fire is gone. The shadows look like the figure. She needs the window.

They decide to speak to her. They ask where she is, who the “shadow” is, who Thomas might be. She answers in fragments: “Your shadow… the birds… the fire.” She demands matches. She demands space. “Move, please.” They debate whether this is a dream or a nightmare. One consults a symbol list: birds can mean freedom, new beginnings, a message. The room and shadows signal repression. Maybe, they say, she remembers who she was. Maybe she’s only a conduit.

Her distress rises when they test her reactions. A lighter flares: too bright. She screams. Shadows explode into glare. Someone sketches a bird to soothe her, but the drawing is wrong. She thrashes against the restraints, her words breaking: “Thomas. Fire. Birds. My—” The room jolts into emergency. They shout to pull the extraction charge. A colleague is too slow. In the aftermath, someone is dead. The charge is finally pulled.

A formal voice cuts in: “All future LYDIA experiments must cease immediately.” The lead insists they were close. LYDIA-4D17 answered more coherently than any prior subject. With stronger restraints and more staff to keep everyone safe, he argues, a breakthrough is possible. The authority relents: two weeks to change his mind. Return to the dig site. Find another subject.

A new team is ushered into a lab, suited up, guided to an observation deck. The project is introduced with clean acronyms and promised fortunes. “LYDIA,” they’re told, isn’t a person but a process: Life-Yielding, Death-Integrated Animation. Reanimation was impossible until six months ago. Twelve subjects have failed for reasons the team now labels “mental anomalies”—from vicious, vivid dreams to near-flat brainwaves. On the table lies LYDIA-5D18, taken from an undisclosed location. A newcomer stares and says softly, “This is a person.” The answer is cold: She was a person. Now she is an empty shell, a conduit. If they succeed, their salaries will be multiplied “to the greatest of extremities.”

The story holds to the woman’s voice even as the lab tries to erase it. She does not accept the name they give her, rejects “Lydia,” and calls for Thomas with terror and love. She begs for fire to burn away the shadows, begs for birds to come in from the snow. The scientists frame her vision as data and caution at first, then as a problem to be solved by force. They ignore what her symbols plainly ask for—freedom, warmth, recognition—and chase a breakthrough that treats memory and grief as glitches. The doors open again. The next subject is wheeled in. The test begins.
